It depends, To me, the second one is better since it's simple to remember you need a Node instance, and in this case, you only have a `relay.Node` instance but maybe you want to implement a custom behavior and you create a new `class Node` so you only need to change the import statement, one of the pros you only import the class Node.

For others, the first is better since `Namespaces are one honking great idea`, so when you read your code again you can see what kind of Node instance is, then it helps to omit to go to the import section since it's more readable, the con is you are importing the whole module but you only use one class of that package and this approach is more acceptable for standard libraries than custom.

So not only one is "correct", both are, think about what is better for you. and use that style.

If you want to clarify what is your style [import statements](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#imports) section from pep8 can help you.

A common naming/import pattern is the following:

Extension module: `_mymodule.so`
Python module or package: `mymodule.py` or `mymodule/__init__.py`

Where the `__init__.py` file contains:
```python
from _mymodule import *
```

This way the main `mymodule` namespace has access to all of the function/classes from the extension module.

@ArchangeGabriel: The fact that you have to do `chown` from outside of certbot is not certbot's fault (or even relevant to certbot per se). Rather,  it is an obvious consequence of your conscious choice of not running certbot as root. This is different for many other users who run certbot as root (which, as far as I understand, is an intended primary _m.o._).

A quick digression: Most *nix systems do not allow ownership giveaway "for obvious [security reasons](http://unix.stackexchange.com/questions/27350/why-cant-a-normal-user-chown-a-file)"
So, a user can `chown` only from and to the user (and the group) whose effective permissions you have. Thus, a non-root user can `chown` only to a different group that he also belongs to.
AFAIR, all BSD's are like that, but some System V OSes, e.g. IRIX, did allow ownership giveaway and that was a problem.

I think the cause of these problems is a mix of Python packages provided from your package manager and `pip`. [We strongly recommend](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/install.html#installing-from-source) you don't `pip install` packages like this as it can corrupt packages on your system and results in problems like this.

`certbot-auto` works around the problem by minimizing it's reliance on packages installed elsewhere on your system. Our [PPA](https://launchpad.net/~certbot/+archive/ubuntu/certbot) is unaffected by this problem as it includes a version of `six` new enough to avoid these issues.

If you don't think this is the cause of your problems, I'm happy to look into it, however, I'd like to know what supported installation method you used that resulted in a newer version of `six` not being installed.

I looked into the issue some more and found the source of the bug.

Ray starts the local scheduler with some initial number of available workers which usually matches the number of CPUs. Creating an actor uses a worker, but destroying the actor does not add a worker back to the available workers. So after the initial pool of workers is depleted, new actors ask the local scheduler to create a new worker which causes the overhead.

I'm learning against supporting `GetChannel.clone` for now... in addition to being poorly motivated, there's a possible source of confusion: if I have two clones of the same `GetChannel`, and put one item into the channel, then does it get sent to *both* clones (fork) or just one of them (round-robin)?

I'm also being tempted to make `GetChannel.close` raise an error if it's closed while there are still pending items, and `GetChannel.__del__` issue a warning in the same case. Maybe if we do this then we'd let people do `get_channel.close(ignore_pending=True)` when they want to intentionally throw away pending data and don't want to bother putting a `try`/`except` around things.

I'm also wondering if we should try making this API start out relatively minimal. I don't really want to add `qsize` and `full` and all that stuff that `Queue` has, it's mostly useless and encourages bad habits. I'm tempted to even remove `put_nowait` and `get_nowait` until someone has a use case.

Still not sure what to do about the `always_abort` thing. I don't like the way it works here. Some options:

* Move the flag to `reschedule` instead of `wait_task_rescheduled`?
* Give up on tracking the relationship between calls to `put` and `PutHandle` objects
* Hold our noses and put up with the hacky version I started with, where the `PutHandle._tasks` set could be slightly out-of-sync with reality and `PutHandle.close` had to catch this and work around it.

And finally I am wondering about how to roll this out. The idea would be to eventually replace `trio.Queue`, which is heavily used. There are a lot of subtle design questions here (as you can see above), so it makes me nervous that this PR hasn't attracted any feedback yet :-). Maybe the way to do it is to have a release where we advertise channels as [provisional](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0411/), without yet deprecating `trio.Queue`, and then hassle folks to try it out and give feedback? (And then if it passes, the next release could deprecate `trio.Queue` in favor of channels.)

@pquentin points out that one pretty self-contained thing that needs doing on the urllib3 branch, and that might be a good place for someone to start if they're motivated and familiar with python in general but need some time to get up to speed on that project, would be to split out the source-code transformation plugin. Currently it lives inside `setup.py` in that branch, but really it should be a standalone project, so that other people can use it too by just importing it into their `setup.py`. And there's some other fairly straightforward maintenance that would also make sense, like writing some tests for the code transformation, and updating it to work on python 2.

(Making it work on python 2 is fairly straightforward: the code uses the `tokenize` module to break up Python source into tokens, and then iterates through applying certain rules to certain tokens, in particular the `async` and `await` tokens. On Python 3, `tokenize` classifies those tokens as being "keyword" tokens, while on python 2 they're plain "names". Right now the code only recognizes them if they're "keywords". But it would be pretty easy to relax this rule.)

All the places we use it in trio we *don't* want this kind of collapsing. We want to take an arbitrary function, wrap its outcome in an object, move that object somewhere else, and then unwrap it again, and have this whole process be perfectly transparent. Collapsing nested `Outcome`s would break this transparency, since "arbitrary function" includes those that return `Outcome` objects.

Example: `trio.run_sync_in_worker_thread (capture, some_func)` should return an `Outcome`, but wouldn't if we did collapsing.

Wall of text incoming!

> Each model proto should be associated with one version.

This is true in the current proposal, c.f. https://github.com/onnx/onnx/commit/033b810a0a4a3d7e79ff083b2e994fd78549d5fb#diff-55a1ff6cfca1e1c4845ce400a29d1557R117

> I agree on the point that operator set may be changed a little bit more frequently at the beginning, but that should not last for too long, right?

I think, looking at the history of all sorts of standards (HTML, CSS, TLS, C++, TCP, ...), features designed specifically to help the standard evolve in the future were never regretted. (This is how you get things like https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-davidben-tls-grease-01 and https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-thomson-postel-was-wrong-00)

> For goal "Don't break models", I vote for "fail with a message stating that the model version is not supported by current onnx lib".

and

> Went thru the proposals again. Are we willing to have VersionN to be able to handle Version1, Version2, ..., VersionN-1 model please?

Here's the argument from end results: I think that if you DON'T have this property, then you will end up in one of two possible end states: (1) we build tooling that can go from Version N to Version N+1, so that models can be upgraded or (2) it is so painful to bump a version number (because everyone's already exported models stop working) that we will never update the version number.

Here's the argument, "it's not a huge burden on backends." Suppose that previous version of the Caffe2 importer supported Version 1 to Version N-1. Then it doesn't seem like a big ask for them to keep that import logic, and just add new logic to support the new operators. So there's some nuance here: if you're working on a new importer, you should decide the earliest version you care about, and then not bother to implement support for earlier models. And I think that is fine. But assuming you have support, it's not that much work to carry it forward.

> To clarify, this does not mean new operator definitions will be added for extensions of existing operators, am I right?

Any extension must be strictly backwards compatible. I think, in practice, this means that you are only allowed to add new optional attributes (this has the added benefit that a framework can give an obvious message when it doesn't understand an attribute); no semantics changes (even loosening of restrictions) are allowed.

> Assuming operator definitions refer to the ops registered in defs.cc, where would the set of valid operator names live?

We would add a new file defining the changelog https://github.com/onnx/onnx/commit/033b810a0a4a3d7e79ff083b2e994fd78549d5fb#diff-55a1ff6cfca1e1c4845ce400a29d1557R117 which in turn would define the current mapping. We'd update our documentation scripts to generate a separate documentation page for each version of the ONNX operator definition.

> What's the benefit of having this indirection (operator name + version == operator definition)

The benefit is that it allows us to reallocate valuable names like `Add` or `MatMul` if we truly need to make BC-breaking changes to them (indeed, `Add` is particularly poignant because we are likely change this in the new future, to fix our broadcasting strategy.) Why is it important to reallocate valuable names? Because some definitions of functions are *actively harmful*, and we need to discourage their usage, even if their name sounds attractive. I can give plenty of examples from C (where we are *never* allowed to allocate new names): [strncpy](https://randomascii.wordpress.com/2013/04/03/stop-using-strncpy-already/), [gets](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1694036/why-is-the-gets-function-so-dangerous-that-it-should-not-be-used), [Java antipatterns](http://www.odi.ch/prog/design/newbies.php).

"But wait", you might say, "ONNX is *different*, it's not as complicated as C; we won't have problems like this." OK, so here's another example: Slice. We seeded this operator by taking its definition directly from Caffe2, https://caffe2.ai/docs/operators-catalogue.html#slice . See anything weird about it? It treats indices as exclusive, UNLESS they are negative, in which case they are inclusive. This is not very clear from the English, "If a negative value is passed for any of the start or end indices, it represent number of elements before the end of that dimension.", but it is clear from the example.  Now, ONNX had the same verbiage, but with an extra comment that it's consistent with Numpy; but Numpy ALWAYS treats ending indices as exclusive

So let's say you messed it up, and the reference implementations have Caffe2 semantics because no one was looking too closely. Now you have a walking footgun waiting to happen for anyone implementing export/input on slice. You NEED to deprecate the old definition for Slice, and put something in that people are less likely to misuse.

> Or are we assuming there is no release cadence here? IR/op versions can be updated anytime?

Releases are useful markers for external users who cannot be bothered to keep track of every commit that goes in a repo, but we need to make sure our story is also consistent with users who are tracking ONNX HEAD (and this is more likely than you might think, because a lot of fixes we are putting into ONNX these days are driven by real world use cases, and the only way for someone to get such a fix is to track master.) So one thing I like about my proposal is that it is compatible with an ever evolving master branch. ONNX version is public relations, but ONNX operator version is *semantics*. Makes sense to decouple them.

> it would be nice to have the versioning convey some indication of stability and also tell us what the compatibility story is. 

Right; so, I guess the point you are making here is that we want the global operator version to carry some public relations semantics, but the global operator version as I've described above is purely a namespace control mechanism; e.g., you bump it whenever you make a BC breaking change, not when you make a model update.

So let me propose the following correspondence between ONNX version (the thing we publically release and tell everyone about) and the global ONNX operator version (an internal thing, which is exported to the model proto, and is used to actually make everything technically work):

* Every ONNX release is associated with an ONNX operator version; basically, whatever the operator version in the repo was at the time of release.

* A backend is said to **fully support** an ONNX release if it is guaranteed to be able to load any model exported for that release. Formally, an ONNX release x.y (with global operator version N) is fully supported, if it is shipped with (a) an ONNX changelog that records ONNX operator changes up to version N or greater (N+1, if we permit accretion into the namespace; this may be a case for a minor version number), and (b) implements EVERY operator in the namespace corresponding to global operator version N.

* A backend is said to **support** an ONNX release if, assuming that all of the operators in a model are implemented in the framework, it will successfully load a model. This is distinguished from full support, because a framework may have a few operators that it doesn't support; but as long as a model doesn't actually use those operators, everything is fine. Formally, only condition (a) from above must be fulfilled (it's important to note that the changelog MUST be up to date to this version, because otherwise the framework cannot know if some operator was actually changed in a BC-incompatible way.)

* A frontend is said to **target** an ONNX release if it creates a model that will be loadable by backends that support that release. Formally, this means that the model they export has a global operator version N corresponding to this ONNX release (or an earlier version, assuming that version has an equivalent mapping).

I feel that this is a *good* compatibility story, one that avoids needlessly breaking models when breaking changes occur. The global operator version is a technical device that we don't actively publicize, but still exists in the protobuf (and we published detailed mappings letting people correlate operator versions with ONNX release versions.)

----

Thanks for reading, your comments are all helpful!
